content.problemStatement:
Chef has an 8×88 \times 88×8 chessboard. He placed a knight on the square (X1,Y1)(X_1, Y_1)(X1​,Y1​). Note that, the square at the intersection of the ithi^{th}ith row and jthj^{th}jth column is denoted by (i,j)(i, j)(i,j).

Chef wants to determine whether the knight can end up at the square (X2,Y2)(X_2, Y_2)(X2​,Y2​) in **exactly** 100100100 moves or not.

For reference, a knight can move to a square which is:


One square horizontally and two squares vertically away from the current square, or
One square vertically and two squares horizontally away from the current square
A visual description of this may be found here.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first and only line of each test case contains 444 integers X1,Y1,X2,Y2X_1, Y_1, X_2, Y_2X1​,Y1​,X2​,Y2​ — where (X1,Y1)(X_1, Y_1)(X1​,Y1​) denotes the starting square of the knight and (X2,Y2)(X_2, Y_2)(X2​,Y2​) denotes the ending square of the knight.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if knight can move from (X1,Y1)(X_1, Y_1)(X1​,Y1​) to (X2,Y2)(X_2, Y_2)(X2​,Y2​) in **exactly** 100100100 moves. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four integers X1 Y1 X2 Y2 denoting the starting and ending squares of the knight (1 ≤ X1,Y1,X2,Y2 ≤ 8).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the knight can reach (X2,Y2) from (X1,Y1) in exactly 100 moves, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: start and end are the same square (same colour) → YES.\nTest case 2: colours differ (16 even vs 13 odd) → NO.\nTest case 3: both squares are white (even parity) → YES.

solution.approach: A knight always changes the colour of the square it stands on after each move because its move vector (2,1) has odd Manhattan distance. Therefore after an even number of moves the knight ends on a square of the same colour as the start, and after an odd number of moves on the opposite colour. The board is small (8×8) and the knight graph is connected, meaning any square of the same colour can be reached in at most 6 moves. Since 100 is a large even number, the only condition that matters is colour parity. So for each test case we simply compare (X1+Y1) mod 2 with (X2+Y2) mod 2. If they are equal, answer is YES, otherwise NO.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
